column2: ISB Joint Coordinate System. Anchors every joint frame + sign convention. Keeps the joint sheet coherent with the Dumas segment frames the BSIP values already live in.
Reed et al. (1999) regressions for trunk centres (CJC, TJC, SJC, LJC). Female coefficients already completed in your Dumas (2007) appendix — pull from there, not Reed directly.
Harrington et al. (2007) for the HJC. Better-validated, pelvic-geometry driven. Reed↔Harrington HJC offset (few mm) logged as a deviation, not silently absorbed.
Knee/ankle/elbow/wrist = landmark midpoints (epicondyles, malleoli, styloids), per ISB SCS definitions — not regressions.
Soucie et al. (2011), female-inclusive. Boone & Azen (1979) secondary/historical (male-only — population mismatch).
Winter (level gait, you have it); Riener et al. (2002) for stair descent/ascent; task kinematics per movement.

Joint frames MUST match the Dumas SCS the BSIP tensors are expressed in. A joint frame defined differently than its segment frame = every inertia number off by a transform. This is the whole reason ISB was chosen.

ISB Cardan/Euler per joint. Grood–Suntay floating-axis convention: e1 = flexion axis fixed in proximal segment; e3 = long axis fixed in distal segment; e2 = floating axis (e3 × e1), carries ab/adduction.
+ flexion, + adduction, + internal rotation (right side); mirror for left. State explicit per joint in the Definitions sheet — do not assume.
Anatomical neutral standing = all joint angles zero.
Angles in degrees. JC coordinates in mm, expressed in the PROXIMAL segment frame. ROM in degrees.
Soucie = mixed-sex, ages 2–69. Boone & Azen = male only. Reed/Dumas = young adult. Harrington = mixed adults+children, MRI. Document, don't assume median.
